name: Initialize Playwright with TypeScript
description: Set up Playwright Test from scratch with TypeScript, configure browsers, create your first test, and run it across multiple browsers.
trendingReason: Playwright has become the leading E2E testing framework, surpassing Cypress in adoption

steps.title: Initialize Playwright in your project
steps.description: Run the Playwright installer to scaffold config, example tests, and download browsers.
steps.tip: The installer asks interactive questions. Choose TypeScript, tests/ directory, and yes to GitHub Actions workflow for CI-ready setup.

steps.title: Install specific browsers
steps.description: Download only the browsers you need to save disk space.

steps.title: Run the example test
steps.description: Execute the scaffolded tests to verify the setup works.

steps.title: Run tests with the UI mode
steps.description: Open the interactive test runner for visual debugging.
steps.tip: UI mode provides a time-travel debugger, live locator picker, and watch mode for rapid test development.

steps.title: View the HTML report
steps.description: Open the built-in test report to review results and traces.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Playwright Test Setup
set -euo pipefail

PROJECT_DIR=${1:-.}

echo "=== Initializing Playwright ==="
cd "$PROJECT_DIR"

# Install Playwright
npm install -D @playwright/test

# Install browsers
npx playwright install chromium firefox webkit

# Create config if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -f "playwright.config.ts" ]; then
cat > playwright.config.ts << 'CONFIG'
import { defineConfig, devices } from '@playwright/test';

export default defineConfig({
  testDir: './tests',
  fullyParallel: true,
  forbidOnly: !!process.env.CI,
  retries: process.env.CI ? 2 : 0,
  workers: process.env.CI ? 1 : undefined,
  reporter: 'html',
  use: {
    baseURL: 'http://localhost:3000',
    trace: 'on-first-retry',
    screenshot: 'only-on-failure',
  },
  projects: [
    { name: 'chromium', use: { ...devices['Desktop Chrome'] } },
    { name: 'firefox', use: { ...devices['Desktop Firefox'] } },
    { name: 'webkit', use: { ...devices['Desktop Safari'] } },
  ],
});
CONFIG
fi

# Create example test
mkdir -p tests
cat > tests/example.spec.ts << 'TEST'
import { test, expect } from '@playwright/test';

test('homepage loads successfully', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.goto('/');
  await expect(page).toHaveTitle(/.+/);
});
TEST

echo ""
echo "=== Setup Complete ==="
echo "Config:  playwright.config.ts"
echo "Tests:   tests/"
echo ""
echo "=== Commands ==="
echo "Run tests:    npx playwright test"
echo "UI mode:      npx playwright test --ui"
echo "Show report:  npx playwright show-report"

faq.question: Which browsers does Playwright support?
faq.answer: Playwright supports Chromium (Chrome, Edge), Firefox, and WebKit (Safari). It ships its own browser binaries to ensure consistent behavior across environments. You can also test against branded browsers like Chrome and Edge.

faq.question: How much disk space do browsers require?
faq.answer: Each browser is roughly 150-300 MB. Install only the browsers you need with 'npx playwright install chromium' instead of all three. Use PLAYWRIGHT_BROWSERS_PATH to share browsers across projects.

faq.question: Can I use Playwright with JavaScript instead of TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. Playwright works with both JavaScript and TypeScript. During init, choose JavaScript when prompted. You can also create .spec.js files alongside TypeScript ones in the same project.
